label: ai
topic: What COP21 is and why Paris
text:
The twenty-first Conference of the Parties to the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change, held in Paris in late November and December of 2015, represented the most ambitious attempt yet to forge a truly universal climate agreement. Previous summits had produced the Kyoto Protocol, which bound only industrialised nations to emissions targets, leaving the world's largest and fastest-growing emitters under no comparable obligation. The Paris conference sought to close that gap by persuading every country, developed and developing alike, to submit its own nationally determined contribution to emissions reduction.

The choice of Paris was partly symbolic: France carries considerable diplomatic weight as a permanent Security Council member and had invested enormous political capital in ensuring the summit would not collapse the way Copenhagen did in 2009, when last-minute walkouts and competing draft texts left delegates without a formal agreement. The French presidency, led by Laurent Fabius, ran a notably disciplined process, circulating clean negotiating texts and keeping ministers engaged through the final sleepless weekend of talks. French diplomats spent much of 2015 visiting capitals and building informal alliances between blocs that had historically viewed each other with suspicion, particularly the European Union and the major emerging economies.

The summit also took place under extraordinary security conditions. Two weeks before the opening ceremony, coordinated terrorist attacks across Paris killed one hundred and thirty people. The French government placed the country under a state of emergency, banned large public demonstrations, and deployed thousands of additional security personnel around the Le Bourget conference site. Despite these pressures, the summit proceeded on schedule, and many observers noted that the attacks, paradoxically, sharpened diplomatic focus and reduced the appetite for political theatrics that had derailed earlier conferences.

COP21 brought together not only national delegations but also subnational governments, businesses, and civil society organisations in an unprecedented series of side events and pledges. Cities and regions committed to their own emissions targets, and corporate leaders announced investments in clean energy that ran into the hundreds of billions of dollars. This broader mobilisation was a deliberate part of the French strategy: by demonstrating that momentum existed beyond the formal negotiating rooms, the presidency hoped to create political cover for ministers to agree to more ambitious language than their domestic constituencies might otherwise have tolerated. The gamble paid off. On the evening of 12 December 2015, after thirty-six hours of continuous negotiation, Fabius brought down the gavel on the Paris Agreement to sustained applause from the assembled delegates.
